articles.title: Beginner's Guide to Life Jackets and PFDs
articles.summary: Learn how to choose, fit, and maintain a life jacket so every passenger on board stays safe.
articles.content: This beginner-focused guide explains the different types of life jackets (PFDs), how to read the labels, and how to choose the right size for adults and children. It also covers inspection checklists and when to replace your gear.

articles.title: 7 Essential Right-of-Way Rules for New Boaters
articles.summary: Understand who goes first on the water so you can avoid close calls and collisions.
articles.content: This article breaks down seven core right-of-way rules using simple language and diagrams. You’ll learn how to approach other boats, what navigation lights mean, and how to pass or overtake safely.

articles.title: Docking Without Drama: Basic Dock Maneuvers for Beginners
articles.summary: Step-by-step docking tips that help new boaters avoid bumps, scrapes, and panic at the marina.
articles.content: Learn how to plan your approach, use wind and current to your advantage, and communicate with crew while docking. Includes a simple pre-docking checklist you can follow every time.

articles.title: Fueling Your Boat Safely: A Quick Guide for First-Time Owners
articles.summary: Avoid spills, fires, and fumes with these simple fueling steps every beginner should know.
articles.content: This guide covers where to position passengers, how to ground the nozzle, and what to do before and after you fuel. It also explains common fueling mistakes and how to prevent them.

articles.title: Cold Water Survival Basics for Small Boat Operators
articles.summary: Understand the risks of cold water immersion and what to do if someone falls overboard.
articles.content: This article explains the stages of cold water shock, how to use the Heat Escape Lessening Position (HELP), and what gear you should always have ready when boating in cold conditions.

articles.title: Thunderstorm Emergency Actions for Boats Under 26 ft
articles.summary: Step-by-step actions to take before, during, and after a thunderstorm in a small boat.
articles.content: Learn how to spot building thunderstorms, when to head for shelter, and what to do if you are caught on open water in a boat under 26 ft. Includes radio call templates and a quick storm checklist.

articles.title: Updated Thunderstorm Emergency Checklist for Small Powerboats (Under 26 ft)
articles.summary: The latest, field-tested thunderstorm emergency checklist tailored to open boats under 26 ft.
articles.content: This updated guide refines earlier thunderstorm procedures with clearer steps, revised VHF radio scripts, and new advice for using smartphone radar apps on inland lakes and rivers.

articles.title: Thunderstorm Preparation for Cruising Boats 26–40 ft
articles.summary: How to secure a larger cruising boat and crew before severe weather arrives.
articles.content: Focuses on line management, securing canvas, electronics protection, and anchoring considerations for boats between 26 and 40 feet in length.

articles.title: How to Make a Mayday, Pan-Pan, or Sécurité Call on VHF
articles.summary: Step-by-step scripts for making distress, urgency, and safety calls on your VHF radio.
articles.content: Covers channel selection, how to format a Mayday, Pan-Pan, and Sécurité call, and what information to include so rescuers can find you quickly.

articles.title: Night Navigation Basics: Safe Boating After Dark
articles.summary: Learn how to read lights, buoys, and charts when navigating at night or after dark.
articles.content: This guide explains how to interpret navigation lights, use range markers, dim onboard lighting, and adjust speed for safe night passages. Includes a pre-departure night checklist.

articles.title: Reading Daymarks and Buoys: A Visual Guide
articles.summary: A photo-heavy guide to recognizing common daymarks and buoys on inland waters and coastal routes.
articles.content: Walks through lateral markers, safe water marks, isolated danger marks, and special purpose buoys with examples and practice scenarios.

articles.title: Beginner Anchoring: Setting Your First Hook
articles.summary: A beginner-friendly walkthrough of choosing an anchor spot, setting the hook, and checking that you’re holding.
articles.content: Explains scope, bottom types, how to drop and set the anchor, and how to back down gently to ensure it holds. Includes diagrams and a simple checklist.

articles.title: Advanced Anchoring Techniques for Crowded Harbors
articles.summary: Stern anchors, med-mooring, and other advanced anchoring techniques for tight spaces.
articles.content: Covers setting multiple anchors, reducing swing, and managing lines safely when space is limited or winds shift.

articles.title: VHF Radio Basics: Making Clear Calls on the Water
articles.summary: Get comfortable with your VHF radio, from channel selection to routine and emergency calls.
articles.content: Explains key VHF channels, how to conduct a radio check, and how to structure common calls, including requesting a bridge opening or assistance.

articles.title: Using DSC on Your VHF: Digital Distress and Position Calls
articles.summary: Learn how to register, configure, and use Digital Selective Calling (DSC) features on modern VHF radios.
articles.content: Covers MMSI registration, wiring GPS to your radio, sending test calls, and initiating a DSC distress alert.

articles.title: Pre-Departure Safety Checklist for Any Small Boat
articles.summary: A quick, repeatable pre-departure check that works for most small powerboats and pontoons.
articles.content: Outlines a simple walk-through of your boat before leaving the dock, including equipment checks, passenger briefing, and weather review.

articles.title: What I Learned from My First Overnight on the Hook
articles.summary: A personal story from a new boater about mistakes, surprises, and small victories during a first overnight anchorage.
articles.content: Blends narrative with practical takeaways about gear to bring, how to manage nerves, and what to double-check before turning in for the night.

checklists.title: Pontoon Day Trip Safety Checklist
checklists.description: A step-by-step safety checklist for a 4–8 hour pontoon outing with family or friends.

checklists.title: Open Motorboat Half-Day Fishing Checklist
checklists.description: Gear and safety checks for a short fishing trip on an inland lake or river.

checklists.title: Family Day Trip Checklist for Open Motorboats
checklists.description: Packing, safety, and comfort items for a full-day outing with kids on an open runabout.

checklists.title: Sailboat Weekend Cruise Safety Checklist
checklists.description: Safety gear, rigging checks, and provisions for a weekend aboard a cruising sailboat.

checklists.title: Multi-Day Passage Planning Checklist for Sailboats
checklists.description: Planning, safety, and redundancy checks for multi-day offshore passages.

checklists.title: Personal Watercraft Quick Ride Checklist
checklists.description: Helmet, PFD, lanyard, and local rules checklist for short PWC rides.

checklists.title: Pontoon Evening Cruise Lighting & Safety Checklist
checklists.description: Checks for navigation lights, visibility, and passenger safety on an evening pontoon cruise.

checklists.title: Open Boat Overnight Camping Checklist
checklists.description: Safety and camping gear checklist for tying up at a dock or beach overnight in a small open boat.

courses.title: Lakeside Basic Boating Safety
courses.description: A one-day, in-person class covering essential boating safety, required equipment, navigation rules, and local regulations. Ideal for first-time boat owners.

courses.title: Harborview Basic Boating Safety Course
courses.description: Evening classroom sessions plus on-the-water practice focused on operating small powerboats safely on inland lakes and rivers.

courses.title: Online Basic Boating Safety (State-Approved)
courses.description: Self-paced online course that meets most state boating safety education requirements. Includes quizzes and a printable completion certificate.

courses.title: Yacht Club Basic Powerboat Safety
courses.description: Club-led basic safety course with a focus on family day-boating, line handling, and docking practice for small cruisers and pontoons.

courses.title: City Parks Basic Boating Safety Program
courses.description: Comprehensive beginner safety course offered through the city parks department, including classroom instruction and a proctored exam.

courses.title: Intensive Basic & Intermediate Powerboat Safety
courses.description: A fast-paced course combining basic and intermediate safe boat handling. Best suited for those who already have some experience at the helm.

courses.title: Coastal Basic Boating Safety Weekend
courses.description: A coastal-focused weekend safety course that includes both classroom time and coastal navigation exercises.

courses.title: Advanced Coastal Navigation for Cruisers
courses.description: In-depth course on tides, currents, pilotage, and coastal passage planning for experienced skippers.

courses.title: Inland Chart Reading & GPS Basics
courses.description: Learn to read inland charts, interpret symbols, and cross-check your GPS for safer navigation on lakes and rivers.

courses.title: VHF Marine Radio Operator Course
courses.description: Covers VHF radio regulations, proper calling procedures, DSC features, and practice with simulated radio traffic.

courses.title: Onboard First Aid & CPR for Boaters
courses.description: Teaches CPR, bleeding control, hypothermia treatment, and common onboard medical emergencies with a boating focus.

courses.title: Boat Trailer Handling & Ramp Etiquette
courses.description: Hands-on course focused on backing a trailer, launching and retrieving safely, and avoiding common ramp mistakes.

glossary_terms.definition: The international radio distress signal used only when a vessel or person is in grave and imminent danger and immediate assistance is required.
A radio urgency signal indicating a serious problem that is not immediately life-threatening, such as a mechanical breakdown or a minor injury.
A radio safety signal used to broadcast important safety information, such as weather warnings or navigation hazards, to other vessels.
Very High Frequency radio used for marine communications, including distress calls, bridge openings, and routine traffic between boats and shore stations.
A feature of modern VHF radios that allows boaters to send automated digital distress alerts and direct calls using a unique MMSI number.
The waves created by a boat as it moves through the water, which can affect other vessels, shorelines, and docks.
The widest point of a boat’s hull, measured from side to side.
The vertical distance between the waterline and the deepest part of a boat’s hull, indicating how much water depth the boat requires.
The vertical distance from the waterline to the lowest point of the boat’s deck or gunwale.
The ratio of anchor rode (line and chain) length to the depth of the water plus the boat’s freeboard, used to judge how much rode to deploy when anchoring.

tools.name: Boat Capacity Calculator
tools.description: Interactive calculator that estimates a safe maximum passenger capacity based on boat dimensions, type, and water type. Allows saving results into trip plans.

tools.name: Engine Maintenance Scheduler
tools.description: Guided tool for creating a recurring maintenance schedule based on engine type, usage frequency, and water type, with reminders and saved schedules.

tools.name: Trip Plan Organizer
tools.description: Simple planner for organizing trip details, passengers, and notes, and attaching saved capacities and checklists.

checklist_items.text: Check detailed weather forecast and thunderstorm risk for the entire day trip window.
Confirm passenger count (4–6 people) and verify it does not exceed the boat’s capacity plate.
Ensure a properly sized, USCG-approved life jacket is available and in good condition for each passenger.
Test navigation lights, horn, and any installed VHF radio or mobile communication devices.
Verify fuel level is sufficient for the trip plus a safety reserve and check for fuel or oil leaks.
Maintain a safe speed, obey no-wake zones, and keep a proper lookout at all times.
Re-count passengers after each stop or swim break and confirm everyone is back on board with PFDs available.
Stow loose gear, secure coolers and bags, and remove any tripping hazards on deck.
Check fuel level and engine gauges, then confirm the route back to the ramp or marina before departing.
Install drain plug, inspect hull for damage, and verify bilge is dry before launching.
Confirm required safety gear is aboard: PFDs, throwable device, sound-producing device, and fire extinguisher.
Periodically check the bilge and engine compartment for fuel odors, leaks, or excessive water.
Give a short safety briefing covering life jackets, staying seated underway, and what to do if someone falls overboard.
Inspect standing and running rigging, sails, and shackles for wear or damage before leaving the dock.
Plot your intended route and at least one alternate, noting safe harbors and fuel stops along the way.
Attach the engine cut-off lanyard (kill switch) to your PFD or wrist before starting the PWC.
Turn on required navigation lights before sunset and verify all bulbs are working and visible from the correct angles.
Inspect anchor, rode, and spare lines, ensuring they are ready for overnight use at your planned anchoring spot.
Confirm navigation lights, flashlight/headlamp, and spare batteries are on board and functioning.
After setting the anchor and any shore lines, verify the boat is not dragging by checking shore reference points over time.
